title: A failed bot-action submit permanently stalls a networked room with a single driving client — lastBotKey is set before the fire-and-forget POST, there is no retry, and bot turns are excluded from the stall watchdog
evidence: In maybeDriveBot the per-phase guard key is committed BEFORE the submit: `this.lastBotKey = key;` (line 1034) then `this.submitAction(action, /* retryOnConflict */ false, actingId);` (line 1047). submitAction is fire-and-forget; on a network rejection its `.catch` (line 801-804) only calls failFire when `!actingPlayerId`, which is false for a bot proxy, so a failed bot submit is silently logged and never retried (key already equals lastBotKey on every subsequent emitState frame, so line 1033 returns). Separately, updateTurnWatch marks a turn watchable only when `!this.botByTank.has(activeId)` (line 973), so a bot's turn gets NO wait/stall banner. When only one client can drive the bot (e.g. a networked room with a single human plus CPU seats), a single transient submit failure freezes the board with zero user-facing signal and no recovery but reload.
impact: Networked games with CPU seats and only one connected human can wedge permanently on a dropped bot submission, with no banner, no retry, and no self-heal. Multi-human rooms are resilient (another client's submit wins), so exposure is the single-driver configuration.
recommendation: On a bot-submit failure, reset lastBotKey (or key it on outcome) so the next frame re-attempts; and/or extend the turn-stall watchdog to cover an active bot seat that has not produced a committed action within the timeout so the user at least gets the leave-to-lobby escape.
acceptance_criteria: A simulated failed bot submit is retried by the same client on a later frame rather than latched off by lastBotKey
A stalled bot turn surfaces a user-visible recovery path (banner or auto-retry) in a single-human networked room
